Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.Overview: Bacterial Spot is a highly contagious bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas pathogens. It is one of the most destructive diseases affecting bell peppers, causing leaf spotting, defoliation, and fruit damage, which heavily reduces crop yield.
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.Symptoms:
• Small, water-soaked, circular green spots appear on the undersides of leaves first.
• Spots mature into dark brown, greasy lesions with raised margins and light yellow halos.
• Infected leaves turn yellow and drop off, causing major defoliation and exposing fruit to sunscald.
• Raised, brown, wart-like lesions with a scab-like appearance develop on the pepper fruits.
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.Causes:
• Caused by the bacterium Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria.
• Spreads rapidly via wind-driven rain, splashing overhead irrigation, contaminated tools, and human contact when foliage is wet.
• Favored by high relative humidity (above 85%) and warm temperatures between 75°F and 90°F (24°C to 32°C).
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.Prevention:
• Always plant certified pathogen-free seeds and transplants from trusted nurseries.
• Rotate crops every 3 years, avoiding fields previously planted with peppers, tomatoes, or eggplants.
• Utilize drip irrigation or soaker hoses instead of overhead sprinklers to keep leaves dry.
• Sanitize all farming tools, pruning shears, stakes, and machinery with disinfectant.
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.Treatment:
• Prune and destroy infected leaves at the first sign of disease. Do not compost infected debris.
• Apply copper-based liquid fungicides/bactericides at 7-10 day intervals during wet periods.
• Use preventative bio-fungicides containing Bacillus subtilis to suppress bacterial colonization.

Pepper__bell___healthy.Overview: The bell pepper plant is healthy and shows no signs of active pathogens, structural damage, or nutritional deficiencies.
Pepper__bell___healthy.Symptoms:
• Leaves are vibrant green, clean, and free of spots, lesions, or powdery growth.
• Stems are sturdy, upright, and free of dark streaks or soft rot.
• Flowers and fruits are developing normally without discoloration or blemishes.
Pepper__bell___healthy.Causes:
• Favorable environmental conditions and good agricultural management practices.
• Proper nutrition, soil balance, and aeration.
Pepper__bell___healthy.Prevention:
• Maintain consistent irrigation (deep watering at the base) to prevent moisture stress.
• Conduct weekly checks of the undersides of leaves for early signs of pests or spotting.
• Apply a balanced N-P-K fertilizer and ensure trace minerals are adequate.
Pepper__bell___healthy.Treatment: • No active chemical treatments or interventions are required. Continue standard cultural practices.

Potato___Early_blight.Overview: Early Blight is a common fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani. It primarily attacks older, mature foliage of potato plants, causing characteristic 'target-board' concentric rings on leaves, leading to premature leaf death and yield reduction.
Potato___Early_blight.Symptoms:
• Small, dark brown to black spots appear on older, lower leaves first.
• Spots enlarge and develop concentric rings, giving them a target-board or bullseye pattern.
• The tissue surrounding the spots turns yellow (chlorotic), and the entire leaf eventually dies and drops.
• Tubers can also develop dark, sunken, leathery lesions.
Potato___Early_blight.Causes:
• Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani, which overwinters in crop debris or soil.
• Spreads by wind, splashing water, insects, and contaminated tools.
• Favored by alternating wet and dry conditions, high humidity, and temperatures around 75°F to 85°F (24°C to 29°C).
Potato___Early_blight.Prevention:
• Plant certified, disease-resistant seed potato varieties.
• Practice a 3-year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops (avoid tomatoes, peppers, and eggplants).
• Apply mulch to prevent soil-borne spores from splashing onto lower leaves.
• Space rows adequately to maximize air circulation and dry the canopy quickly.
Potato___Early_blight.Treatment:
• Remove and bury or burn infected crop residue immediately after harvest.
• Apply protective fungicides (like Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb) when weather conditions favor disease spread.
• Use organic options like copper fungicides or bio-fungicides containing Bacillus subtilis.

Potato___Late_blight.Overview: Late Blight is a devastating oomycete disease caused by Phytophthora infestans (famous for causing the Irish Potato Famine). It spreads extremely fast in cool, wet weather and can destroy entire fields of potatoes and tomatoes within a few days.
Potato___Late_blight.Symptoms:
• Dark, water-soaked, irregular lesions appear on leaf tips or margins, rapidly expanding.
• In humid weather, a white, velvety growth (spores) appears on the undersides of leaves around the lesions.
• Stems develop dark brown to black lesions, causing the plant to collapse.
• Tubers show dark, dry, reddish-brown rot that spreads inwards, rendering them unusable.
Potato___Late_blight.Causes:
• Caused by the water mold (oomycete) Phytophthora infestans.
• Spreads quickly via airborne spores carried by wind for miles, or water runoff.
• Favored by high humidity (90%+), frequent rain or dew, and cool to moderate temperatures (60°F to 75°F / 15°C to 24°C).
Potato___Late_blight.Prevention:
• Plant certified disease-free seed tubers and grow resistant varieties.
• Eliminate volunteer potato plants and cull piles, which serve as sources of infection.
• Avoid overhead watering; schedule watering for early morning so foliage dries quickly.
• Monitor local late blight forecasting networks for early warnings.
Potato___Late_blight.Treatment:
• If Late Blight is detected in a small area, immediately pull up and bag infected plants, then bury or burn them.
• Apply preventative copper fungicides or systemic oomycete-specific fungicides (like Metalaxyl or Fenamidone).
• If the crop is mature and late blight is severe, kill the potato vines (defoliate) to protect tubers before harvest.

Potato___healthy.Overview: The potato plant is healthy, exhibits strong leaf turgor, normal green coloration, and shows no signs of oomycete, viral, or fungal infections.
Potato___healthy.Symptoms:
• Leaves are green, fully expanded, and show no concentric rings, black spots, or white fuzzy mold.
• Stems are strong and green without dark streaks or necrosis.
• Under-soil tubers are firm and free of discoloration or rot.
Potato___healthy.Causes:
• Proper crop rotation, clean seed stock, and ideal environmental balance.
• Healthy soil nutrition, including adequate nitrogen and potassium.
Potato___healthy.Prevention:
• Maintain hilling (covering potato stems with soil) to protect developing tubers from pests and sunlight.
• Ensure uniform soil moisture—avoid letting soil dry out completely, then over-watering.
• Inspect plants regularly, looking specifically for foliar lesions or changes in color.
Potato___healthy.Treatment: • No chemical treatments needed. Continue regular hilling and irrigation.

Tomato_Bacterial_spot.Overview: Bacterial Spot is a major bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas species. It attacks all above-ground parts of the tomato plant, causing dark spots on leaves, stems, and fruits, leading to serious defoliation and unmarketable blemished fruit.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.Symptoms:
• Small, blackish, water-soaked spots with yellow margins appear on leaves.
• Spots dry up, turn brown, and the centers may fall out, giving a shot-hole appearance.
• Extensive spotting causes leaves to yellow, wither, and drop, exposing fruit to sunscald.
• On fruits, spots begin as small, raised, black specks that grow into dark, raised, scabby lesions.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.Causes:
• Caused by bacteria in the genus Xanthomonas (e.g., Xanthomonas perforans).
• Spreads through driving rain, splashing irrigation water, contaminated field machinery, and pruning tools.
• Thrives in wet, hot climates with temperatures of 75°F to 90°F (24°C to 32°C).
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.Prevention:
• Use certified disease-free seed and purchase healthy transplants.
• Keep foliage dry by using drip irrigation or watering at the base of the plant.
• Clean and sanitize stakes, cages, and pruning tools before and after use.
• Do not work in wet fields, as bacteria are easily spread on damp hands and clothing.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.Treatment:
• Apply copper-based bactericides combined with Mancozeb to increase copper efficacy at 7-day intervals.
• Use preventative biological controls such as Bacillus amyloliquefaciens or bacteriophages.
• Remove and destroy highly infected plants immediately to limit spread.

Tomato_Early_blight.Overview: Early Blight is a common fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani. It affects tomatoes worldwide, causing dark lesions with target-board rings on leaves, leading to progressive defoliation from the bottom of the plant upwards.
Tomato_Early_blight.Symptoms:
• Small, brown-to-black spots appear on the older, lower leaves first.
• As spots expand, they develop characteristic concentric circles, resembling a target or bullseye.
• The leaf tissue surrounding the spots turns bright yellow; eventually, leaves wither and fall off.
• Dark, leathery, sunken lesions can form on stems and near the stem-end of the fruit.
Tomato_Early_blight.Causes:
• Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani, which overwinters in crop residues and soil.
• Spreads by wind, rain splash, irrigation, and insect pests.
• Favored by warm temperatures (75°F to 85°F) and wet weather or heavy dew.
Tomato_Early_blight.Prevention:
• Plant resistant tomato varieties and rotate crops every 3 years with non-solanaceous plants.
• Apply a layer of organic mulch around the base of the plant to keep soil from splashing onto leaves.
• Prune lower leaves (up to 12 inches from the ground) once the plant grows to keep foliage off the soil.
• Ensure adequate plant spacing to facilitate rapid drying of leaves.
Tomato_Early_blight.Treatment:
• Spray preventative copper-based fungicides or synthetic fungicides (like Chlorothalonil) starting early in the season.
• Treat with bio-fungicides containing Bacillus subtilis or Trichoderma harzianum.
• Promptly remove and destroy infected lower leaves as soon as spots are detected.

Tomato_Late_blight.Overview: Late Blight is a destructive oomycete disease caused by Phytophthora infestans. It progresses with alarming speed in cool, damp conditions, rotting leaves, stems, and fruits, and can completely kill an entire tomato plant within a few days.
Tomato_Late_blight.Symptoms:
• Large, irregular, water-soaked, olive-green to dark brown lesions appear on leaves.
• A white, fuzzy, mold-like growth forms on the underside of infected leaves during moist or humid periods.
• Dark brown, greasy lesions girdle the stems, causing the branches to break and die.
• Tomato fruits develop large, firm, golden-brown, greasy, wrinkled patches that rot quickly.
Tomato_Late_blight.Causes:
• Caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans.
• Spreads rapidly over large distances via wind-blown spores and water droplets.
• Favored by wet weather (dew, rain, fog) and cool to moderate temperatures of 60°F to 75°F (15°C to 24°C).
Tomato_Late_blight.Prevention:
• Grow disease-resistant cultivars and check transplants carefully before planting.
• Avoid overhead watering and grow tomatoes under cover (such as high tunnels) if possible to keep leaves dry.
• Maintain strict sanitation—clean up all crop debris and cull piles.
• Space plants generously to ensure optimal ventilation.
Tomato_Late_blight.Treatment:
• Bag and destroy infected plants immediately to prevent spores from spreading to neighboring farms.
• Apply preventative copper fungicides weekly during cool, wet weather forecasts.
• Use systemic fungicides specifically rated for oomycetes/late blight (e.g., Propamocarb or Mandipropamid).

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.Overview: Tomato Leaf Mold is a foliar fungal disease caused by Passalora fulva (formerly Cladosporium fulvum). It is especially prevalent in greenhouse-grown tomatoes but can occur outdoors. Unlike other fungi, it only damages leaves and does not cause fruit rot, though it causes heavy defoliation and reduces yield.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.Symptoms:
• Pale green or yellow spots with indefinite margins appear on the upper surface of older leaves.
• An olive-green to purple, velvety mold (spore mass) develops on the corresponding underside of the leaf.
• As the disease progresses, infected leaves turn entirely yellow, curl, wither, and drop prematurely.
• Stems and blossoms can occasionally be infected, but the fruit itself is rarely affected directly.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.Causes:
• Caused by the fungus Passalora fulva.
• Thrives in high relative humidity (above 85%) and moderate temperatures (70°F to 80°F / 21°C to 27°C).
• Spreads via wind-borne spores, splashing water, and contaminated greenhouse tools or structures.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.Prevention:
• Enhance greenhouse ventilation by using fans, vents, and spacing plants widely.
• Select leaf-mold-resistant tomato cultivars.
• Prune suckers and lower foliage to increase air circulation inside the plant canopy.
• Water early in the morning using drip irrigation to ensure foliage dries quickly under sunlight.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.Treatment:
• Remove and safely destroy heavily infected leaves to reduce the spore load.
• Spray preventative copper fungicides or fungicides containing Chlorothalonil when humidity is high.
• Sanitize greenhouse walls, frames, and tools thoroughly between crop cycles using disinfectant.

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.Overview: Septoria Leaf Spot is a very common fungal disease caused by Septoria lycopersici. It affects tomato leaves, starting at the bottom of the plant and moving upwards. While it does not infect the fruit directly, the loss of foliage reduces fruit size and causes sunscald.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.Symptoms:
• Numerous small, circular spots (1/16 to 1/8 inch) appear on lower leaves.
• The spots have dark brown borders and light grey or tan centers.
• Tiny black specks (spore-producing bodies called pycnidia) can be seen in the center of the spots with a magnifying glass.
• Severely infected leaves turn yellow, dry up, and fall off.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.Causes:
• Caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici, which survives on tomato debris and solanaceous weeds.
• Spreads by splashing rain, overhead irrigation, insects, and contaminated tools or clothing.
• Favored by warm temperatures (68°F to 80°F / 20°C to 27°C) and wet leaves.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.Prevention:
• Rotate crops on a 3-year cycle and control weeds (like nightshade) around the field.
• Apply mulch under tomato plants to establish a physical barrier against soil-borne spores.
• Avoid working in tomato patches when the foliage is wet.
• Space plants to allow air to flow freely and dry leaves quickly.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.Treatment:
• Clip off and destroy infected lower leaves to slow the upward climb of the disease.
• Apply copper fungicides or protective fungicides (like Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb) at the first sign of spots.
• Spray biological controls like Bacillus subtilis regularly.

Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.Overview: The Two-Spotted Spider Mite (Tetranychus urticae) is a tiny arachnid pest that sucks plant sap from tomato leaves. Under hot, dry conditions, spider mite populations explode, leading to severe leaf damage, webbing, and plant stunting.
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.Symptoms:
• Leaves show fine yellow speckling or stippling on the upper surface.
• Tiny, silk-like webs appear on the undersides of leaves and at branch joints.
• Leaves turn yellow, bronze, dry out, and drop off in severe infestations.
• Mites look like tiny moving dots (under 1mm) on the undersides of leaves.
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.Causes:
• Caused by feeding of the Tetranychus urticae spider mite.
• Favored by hot (above 85°F), dry, and dusty weather conditions.
• Spreads when mites crawl between touching plants, or drift on wind currents via silk threads.
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.Prevention:
• Keep plants well-watered; drought-stressed plants are highly attractive to spider mites.
• Spray water on dirt pathways to keep dust down, as dusty leaves favor mite colonization.
• Keep natural predators (ladybugs, lacewings, predatory mites) active by avoiding broad-spectrum chemical insecticides.
• Inspect plants regularly, checking leaf undersides for webbing.
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.Treatment:
• Spray plants with a strong blast of water to physically dislodge mites and destroy webs.
• Apply insecticidal soaps, horticultural oils, or neem oil to suffocate the mites (apply in early morning to avoid leaf burn).
• Release commercial predatory mites (e.g., Phytoseiulus persimilis) in greenhouses or gardens.
• In severe outbreaks, apply selective miticides/acaricides (like Abamectin or Spiromesifen).

Tomato__Target_Spot.Overview: Target Spot is a fungal disease caused by Corynespora cassiicola. It affects tomato leaves, stems, and fruits, creating distinct lesions that resemble a target, and can cause significant foliage loss and fruit damage in warm, humid regions.
Tomato__Target_Spot.Symptoms:
• Small, water-soaked spots appear on leaves, expanding to turn light brown with concentric rings.
• Unlike early blight, target spot lesions have a more circular shape and often a prominent yellow border.
• Stems develop dark, elongated lesions.
• On fruits, spots start as small, slightly sunken flecks that grow into large, circular, sunken lesions with concentric rings.
Tomato__Target_Spot.Causes:
• Caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola.
• Thrives in warm temperatures of 70°F to 90°F (21°C to 32°C) combined with high humidity or long periods of leaf wetness.
• Spreads via wind-borne spores and splashing rain.
Tomato__Target_Spot.Prevention:
• Select tolerant tomato cultivars and clear crop residue after harvest.
• Maintain a minimum 3-year crop rotation schedule.
• Stake and prune plants to improve airflow and facilitate dry conditions within the canopy.
• Implement drip irrigation to prevent wetting the foliage.
Tomato__Target_Spot.Treatment:
• Apply protective copper fungicides or synthetic fungicides (such as Azoxystrobin or Boscalid) early in the infection.
• Treat with bio-fungicides to help suppress fungal growth.
• Remove and dispose of infected leaves as soon as they appear.

Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.Overview: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is a highly destructive viral disease transmitted by silverleaf whiteflies. It causes severe stunting, leaf curling, and yellowing, preventing the plant from producing marketable fruit if infected early.
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.Symptoms:
• New leaves are severely stunted and show bright yellow margins.
• Leaf margins curl upward and inward, resembling small cups.
• Plants become severely stunted, upright, and bushy.
• Flowers may drop off before setting fruit, resulting in a dramatic reduction in yield.
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.Causes:
• Caused by the Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (a Begomovirus).
• Transmitted solely by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) as it feeds on plant sap.
• Whiteflies multiply rapidly in hot, dry weather, spreading the virus between hosts.
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.Prevention:
• Grow virus-resistant tomato varieties.
• Control whitefly populations using yellow sticky traps to monitor and catch them.
• Grow tomato seedlings under fine insect netting or floating row covers to exclude whiteflies.
• Remove weed hosts (like nightshades) which serve as viral reservoirs.
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.Treatment:
• There is no cure for viral diseases once a plant is infected. Immediately rogue (pull up) and destroy infected plants.
• Control whitefly vectors using neem oil, insecticidal soaps, or systemic insecticides (like Imidacloprid).
• Release natural predators like parasitic wasps (Encarsia formosa) or green lacewings.

Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.Overview: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV) is a highly infectious and extremely stable plant virus. It causes mottling, leaf distortion, and stunted growth. Because it is highly stable, it can survive on tools, seeds, and soil for years and is easily spread by physical contact.
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.Symptoms:
• Leaves show a distinct mottled pattern of light and dark green areas (a mosaic look).
• Leaf structure may be distorted, narrow, or wrinkled, appearing puckered or fern-like.
• Plants are slightly stunted and have reduced flowering.
• Fruit can develop internal browning and uneven ripening, making them unmarketable.
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.Causes:
• Caused by the Tomato Mosaic Virus (a Tobamovirus).
• Highly contagious and spreads mechanically via hands, tools, stakes, clothing, and plant-to-plant contact.
• Can be seed-borne, infecting seedlings upon germination.
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.Prevention:
• Purchase certified virus-free seeds and heat-treat seeds before planting.
• Plant resistant varieties (look for ToMV resistance codes).
• Wash hands with soap and water and sanitize tools using non-fat dry milk solution or trisodium phosphate (TSP).
• Rotate crops and remove volunteer tomato plants and weeds.
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.Treatment:
• There are no chemical treatments for viral diseases. Infected plants must be pulled up and burned or buried.
• Sanitize all tools and stakes that touched the infected plant immediately.
• Restrict access to the field to prevent spreading the virus to healthy crops on clothes or hands.

Tomato_healthy.Overview: The tomato plant is healthy, showing optimal foliage development, normal color, and strong structural integrity with no signs of disease or pests.
Tomato_healthy.Symptoms:
• Leaves are deep green, fully expanded, and free of spots, margins, yellowing, or abnormal curling.
• Stems are strong, thick, and uniform without dark streaks or soft spots.
• Blossoms and fruits are clean, unblemished, and growing normally.
Tomato_healthy.Causes:
• Ideal environmental parameters, proper plant spacing, balanced nutrition, and irrigation.
• Disease-resistant varieties and clean soil.
Tomato_healthy.Prevention:
• Monitor irrigation carefully, keeping soil moist but not waterlogged to prevent root rot.
• Apply a balanced fertilizer and monitor calcium levels to prevent blossom end rot.
• Keep pruning tools sanitized to maintain health during grooming.
Tomato_healthy.Treatment: • No chemical treatments required. Continue standard cultural maintenance.
